does: This paper provides improved theoretical analysis of online alignment methods for language models, focusing on greedy algorithms like online RLHF and online DPO. The core contribution is proving that these methods achieve constant O(1) cumulative regret under a temperature-zero evaluation criterion that measures only top-ranked responses at inference time, rather than the weaker O(log T) bounds previously established under KL-regularized regret. The key insight is distinguishing between the statistical cost of learning and the randomization introduced by softened training policies, showing that when evaluation focuses on actual deployment behavior (selecting the best response), greedy updates are far more efficient than prior theory suggested.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature analyzing online alignment methods, which emerged as practical alternatives to offline RLHF that continuously improve models through human feedback during deployment. It extends prior theoretical work on KL-regularized regret bounds for greedy online methods, which had provided loose O(log T) guarantees that poorly matched empirical observations. The paper also connects to foundational work in online learning and regret minimization, adapting decision-theoretic frameworks to the alignment setting. It engages with recent developments in online DPO and online RLHF, bringing modern online learning analysis to these contemporary alignment paradigms and reconciling theory with practice.
enables: These results clarify why practitioners find greedy online alignment so effective, potentially encouraging broader deployment of these methods with stronger theoretical confidence. The temperature-zero regret criterion establishes a more realistic performance metric for alignment algorithms that better reflects deployment objectives, potentially inspiring future work to design methods and analyses under this criterion. The separation of learning costs from regularization effects could inform better algorithm design, such as adaptive temperature schedules or hybrid approaches that maintain theoretical efficiency while preserving desired properties of soft policies. This framework may generalize to other supervised learning problems where the training objective (with regularization) diverges from the deployment objective, advancing our theoretical understanding of practical machine learning more broadly.
